title: The 10-Minute Reclaim
subtitle: A daily ritual for taking ten minutes back from the day for the woman, not the mother
excerpt: A ten-minute daily ritual that takes back a small slice of the day for the woman, not the mother. Same ten minutes, same place, same close. Used to keep a thread of the old self alive across the first years of mothering.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. The daily reclaim shape is shared across writing on motherhood, on caregiving, and on the small acts of selfhood that keep a person legible to themselves.

practice.whenToUse: Use daily, ideally at the same time each day. Most workable for new mothers as the first ten minutes after the baby goes down for a nap, or the last ten before bed.

content.text: A ten-minute daily ritual that takes back a small slice of the day for the woman, not the mother. The slice is small enough to find every day. The shape stays the same so the body knows what it is.
New to ritual as a practice? Read
how rituals work
first.
What you will need
Ten minutes. A chair, a corner, a step, a bath. One drink in a real cup. The phone face down or in the other room.
The practice
Prepare
Pick the same ten minutes each day. The same chair, the same drink, the same posture. The repetition is the practice. The body learns to drop in when the chair is sat in.
Release (repeat x3)
I am ready to release the mothering for ten minutes. I release it now. I release it now. I release it now.
Allow (repeat x3)
I am ready to allow ten minutes that are mine. I allow them now. I allow them now. I allow them now.
Integrate

Sit with the drink. The ten minutes can hold whatever you want. A page of a book. A look out of the window. The first ten minutes of a podcast you have been saving. A short walk in the garden. The only rule is that the ten minutes are not for someone else.
Ground it
I am still here, under all the mothering.
Say it once before you stand up. Then carry on with the day.
Where this practice comes from

Original to homemade.education. The daily reclaim shape is shared across writing on motherhood and caregiving. The form here uses the five-part ritual shape (prepare, release, allow, integrate, ground) so the body learns a beginning and a close.
